event: Hunter-gatherer communities, likely ancestral to later Kwa and Mel language speakers, inhabited the forested coast and interior of present-day Liberia.
Iron-working and agricultural societies gradually spread into the region as part of broader West African Iron Age developments.
Migrations brought Mel-speaking peoples such as the Gola and Kissi into the region, among the earliest established ethnic groups in Liberia.
Liberia's coast appeared on European maps such as the Catalan Atlas as trade knowledge of the West African shoreline grew.
Portuguese navigator Pedro de Sintra explored and charted the Liberian coast, naming features along what became known as the Grain Coast.
European traders dubbed the region the 'Grain Coast' for the melegueta pepper (grains of paradise) exported from its shores.
Mande-speaking peoples including the Vai, Mandingo, and Kpelle migrated into the region, reshaping its ethnic and trade landscape.
The American Colonization Society was founded in the United States to resettle free African Americans in West Africa.
U.S. agents secured land at Cape Mesurado from local rulers, establishing the first settlement for freed American slaves.
Freed African Americans founded the settlement of Christopolis, later renamed Monrovia after U.S. President James Monroe.
The colony was formally named Liberia, meaning 'land of the free,' and its capital named Monrovia.
The Vai scholar Momolu Duwalu Bukele developed the Vai syllabary, one of Africa's few indigenous writing systems.
Various American Colonization Society settlements united to form the Commonwealth of Liberia under Governor Thomas Buchanan.
Liberia declared independence, becoming Africa's first independent republic, with Joseph Jenkins Roberts as its first president.
Britain recognized Liberian independence, followed by other European powers, though the United States withheld recognition for years.
The neighboring settler colony of Maryland-in-Africa was annexed into Liberia, expanding its territory.
The United States formally recognized Liberia's independence during the American Civil War.
Liberia's True Whig Party was founded, beginning a political dominance that would last more than a century.
President Edward James Roye was overthrown amid a financial scandal over a ruinous British loan, deepening the nation's debt crisis.
The Berlin Conference and the Scramble for Africa pressured Liberia, which lost territory to British Sierra Leone and French Guinea and Ivory Coast in border disputes.
France forced Liberia to cede contested eastern territory, shrinking the republic's claimed borders.
Liberia faced near-bankruptcy and indigenous uprisings, prompting international intervention to restructure its finances.
The Firestone Tire and Rubber Company secured a one-million-acre rubber concession, making rubber the backbone of Liberia's economy.
A League of Nations inquiry exposed forced-labor and slavery-like practices, forcing the resignation of President Charles D. B. King.
Liberia formally abolished the export of contract labor and reformed administration following international scrutiny.
William V. S. Tubman was elected president, beginning a 27-year tenure marked by his Open Door and Unification policies.
Liberia declared war on the Axis powers and hosted U.S. military facilities, including Roberts Field, during World War II.
Liberia became a founding member of the United Nations.
Women and indigenous property owners gained the right to vote, broadening the franchise beyond the Americo-Liberian elite.
Liberia was a founding member of the Organisation of African Unity, hosting key pan-African conferences.
President Tubman died in office and was succeeded by his vice president, William R. Tolbert.
Master Sergeant Samuel Doe led a military coup, assassinating President Tolbert and ending over a century of Americo-Liberian rule.
Samuel Doe won a widely disputed election, consolidating power amid growing ethnic tensions and repression.
Charles Taylor's National Patriotic Front invaded from Ivory Coast, igniting the First Liberian Civil War.
President Samuel Doe was captured and killed by a rival faction as ECOWAS deployed its ECOMOG peacekeeping force.
A peace agreement ended the first phase of civil war after years of fighting that killed an estimated 200,000 people.
Charles Taylor won the presidential election held after the war, campaigning on a message of restored stability.
Renewed rebellion by the LURD movement plunged the country into the Second Liberian Civil War.
Charles Taylor resigned and went into exile under international pressure, and a comprehensive peace agreement ended the war as UN peacekeepers (UNMIL) deployed.
Ellen Johnson Sirleaf was elected, becoming Africa's first democratically elected female head of state.
A Truth and Reconciliation Commission was established to address atrocities committed during the civil wars.
President Ellen Johnson Sirleaf was awarded the Nobel Peace Prize and won re-election to a second term.
Former president Charles Taylor was convicted of war crimes by a UN-backed court for his role in Sierra Leone's civil war.
A devastating Ebola epidemic struck Liberia, killing thousands and overwhelming the fragile health system.
Former football star George Weah was inaugurated as president, marking Liberia's first peaceful democratic transfer of power in decades.
Joseph Boakai was inaugurated as president after defeating incumbent George Weah, continuing Liberia's democratic transitions.
